answer:
**The Falsafa corpus includes only a single chapter of *Vīramitrodaya* (by Mitra Miśra), titled "Chapter 1".**[^1]

This chapter is a prose digest on calendrical and ritual rules—primarily concerning lunar days (*tithi*), fortnights (bright/dark), day conjunctions (preceding/succeeding), auspicious timings for fasts, *śrāddha*, vows, eclipses, purity periods, and related scriptural citations from Purāṇas and Smṛtis.[^2][^3] It does not contain any section compiling rules on adoption (*dattaka* or *datta-putra*).

Corpus-wide searches for adoption-related terms ("adoption", "adopted son", *datta*, *dattaka*) yield no substantive matches in this work—only a single incidental proper name reference to "Śrīdatta" (an author, unrelated to the topic).[^4] Extensive adoption rules appear instead in other Smṛtis like *Aṅgirasa Smṛti* (e.g., sonship via ritual, *sapiṇḍa* preferences, *gotra* changes).[^5]

*Vīramitrodaya* is a large Medieval Dharmaśāstra digest with sections on topics like inheritance (*dāyabhāga*) and law (*vyavahāra*), where adoption might appear in full editions—but this is absent from the corpus version.[^6]

[^1]: <works/unknown-viramitrodaya-d4b632/wiki/_work.card.md>
[^2]: <works/unknown-viramitrodaya-d4b632/01/translation/#p-4fb38c> (key passage on *tithi* conjunctions).
[^3]: <works/unknown-viramitrodaya-d4b632/wiki/01.full.md> (n-grams, refrains, TextRank all ritual/calendrical).
[^4]: <works/unknown-viramitrodaya-d4b632/01/translation/#p-ff3192>.
[^5]: E.g., <works/unknown-angirasa-smrti-2bf9d1/01/translation/#p-a99f56>.
[^6]: <works/unknown-viramitrodaya-d4b632/meta.json>.
